summary: My real name is Maximilian, but I usually go by Max. I was named after my two grandfathers, Franz-Frank and Josef-Joseph, following a European tradition. I was born in Milwaukee and enlisted in the Navy in 1944 just before my eighteenth birthday.

Franz-Frank was one of my grandfathers, and my name Maximilian was partly given in his honor. He was part of my family's European heritage, which influenced our naming traditions. Although I never met him, his name lives on through me.

Josef-Joseph was my other grandfather, and like Franz, he contributed to my full name, Maximilian. This was a European custom of naming children after ancestors. I carry his name but usually go by Max in daily life.

I enlisted in the United States Navy in July 1944, just before turning eighteen. I initially wanted to serve on submarines but was assigned to the amphibious group instead. I trained at Great Lakes, Fort Pierce, and Newport before serving aboard the USS Hanover during World War II and was later called back during the Korean War.

My basic boot camp started at the Great Lakes Naval Training Station in 1944. It was my first exposure to people from all over the country, outside of Wisconsin. I also joined the Drum & Bugle Corps there, playing trumpet and once volunteering as a bass drummer during a parade at Northwestern University.
cues: people from all over the US

summary: Although I wanted to serve on submarines, I was assigned to the amphibious group after reporting to Great Lakes. I stayed with the amphibious group throughout my five years of service, training extensively in Florida at Fort Pierce and participating in landing craft operations. I was the engineer of a landing craft, taking care of its diesel engine.

My father served as a soldier in the German Army during World War I. He was proud of his service despite being a prisoner of war in France for two years. His experience and strong belief in serving one's country influenced my decision to enlist in the U.S. Navy.

During World War I, my father served in the German Army and was captured, spending two years as a prisoner of war in France. This history was part of my family's legacy and shaped how we viewed military service. It was a stark contrast to my own service in World War II with the U.S. Navy.

France was where my father was held as a prisoner of war during World War I for two years. This experience was a significant part of my family's history. Later, during World War II, I hoped not to be sent to fight in Germany because of my heritage and family ties.

Although I wondered if my German heritage would affect my deployment, I don't believe I was sent to the South Pacific because of it. My service was mostly with the amphibious group, and I trained in various U.S. locations. I was mindful of family members fighting in Europe, like my cousin in the Battle of the Bulge.

My cousin Eugene Fallier fought in the Battle of the Bulge and tragically died on his twenty-first birthday in January 1945. He was an American soldier and my dad's sister's only son. His death deeply affected our family and made me reflect on the complexities of fighting against relatives in war.

Eugene Fallier was my cousin, the only son of my dad's sister. He served as an American soldier in World War II and died during the Battle of the Bulge on his twenty-first birthday, January 20, 1945. His loss was deeply felt by our family, and I often think about how he might have felt fighting against relatives.

My grandfather served in the Austrian Army, and I have a picture of him in uniform. This was part of my family's European military heritage, which included both Austrian and German service. Though I never met him, his service was a point of pride in our family history.

I was part of the 75th Infantry Division during my military service. This unit was part of my family's military tradition, as my grandfather served before me. My own service was mostly naval, but the infantry division connection was part of the broader family story.

I was born and raised in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. My early life there was typical, and I had very little travel experience outside the Midwest until I joined the Navy. Milwaukee was home when my cousin Eugene took me to Mercy Hospital for an emergency appendicitis in 1941.

In 1941, my cousin Eugene took me to Mercy Hospital in Milwaukee for emergency treatment of a ruptured appendix. The doctors said if I hadn't gone then, it might have been fatal. This hospital visit was a significant event in my early life.

After basic training at Great Lakes, my amphibious group went to Fort Pierce, Florida, in October 1944 for two months of specialized amphibious training. We practiced landings with landing craft and did night maneuvers to prepare for potential beach assaults. The barracks there were screened but open-sided due to the warm climate.

Following Fort Pierce, we moved to Newport, Rhode Island, in the winter months of December through February for further training. There, we prepared to join the USS Hanover, a new ship being commissioned. The barracks were enclosed and heated, a stark contrast to Florida's climate.

I was assigned to the USS Hanover, a brand new ship commissioned in Pascagoula, Mississippi. Our ship's company assembled in Newport, Rhode Island, before deployment. I served aboard as the engineer of a landing craft, maintaining its diesel engine during the war.

The USS Hanover was commissioned in Pascagoula, Mississippi, where our ship was built. Although I did not train there, it was the ship's birthplace before we assembled the crew in Newport. Pascagoula was an important location in my naval service story.

I was called back to service during the Korean War from 1950 to 1952. This time, I met new comrades from the East Coast, including two men from New Jersey who were clam lovers. My experience in the Korean War was different from World War II, with a different mix of people and locations.

During my Korean War service, I met two men from New Jersey who were both clam lovers. We often stopped at clam stands whenever we passed through New Jersey. This was a humorous and memorable part of my second military experience.

While at Great Lakes, our Drum & Bugle Corps was invited to march in a parade at Northwestern University. I played bass drum in that parade, even though I had never done it before. It was a memorable event during my early Navy training.

Before joining the Navy, I had never been out of the Midwest except for a trip to Michigan as a child. During my service, I traveled extensively and visited every state but two, Alaska being one of the states I never got to. It's funny how I always skirted around it.

Like Alaska, Oklahoma was one of the two states I never visited during my military service. Despite traveling widely across the United States, I always managed to skirt around Oklahoma. It's one of those quirks of my service record.
